name: Montana Railroad History
description: Interactive map and articles exploring Montana's railroad heritage from the 1880s to today.

articles.title: The Bear Creek Coal Trains
articles.excerpt: The Smith Mine disaster of 1943 killed 74 miners in Carbon County. The Montana, Wyoming and Southern Railroad had been hauling coal from Bear Creek since 1906.

articles.title: The Custer Creek Train Wreck of 1938
articles.excerpt: Flash flooding washed out a bridge over Custer Creek on June 19, 1938. The Milwaukee Road's Olympian passenger train plunged into the gap, killing 47 passengers.

articles.title: The Golden Spike at Gold Creek
articles.excerpt: On September 8, 1883, Henry Villard drove the final spike completing the Northern Pacific Railway at Gold Creek, Montana, in the presence of Ulysses S. Grant.

articles.title: The Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
articles.excerpt: The BA&P was the first major electrified railroad in America, hauling copper ore 26 miles from the Butte mines to the Anaconda smelter beginning in 1913.

articles.title: The Jawbone: Richard Harlow's Impossible Railroad
articles.excerpt: Richard Harlow built the Montana Railroad from Lombard to Lewistown largely on credit and promises, earning it the nickname 'The Jawbone.'

articles.title: The Discovery of Marias Pass
articles.excerpt: John Frank Stevens discovered Marias Pass in December 1889, walking alone in temperatures of -40°F to find the lowest crossing of the Continental Divide.

articles.title: The Milwaukee Road Electrification
articles.excerpt: The Milwaukee Road electrified 440 miles of mountain railroad from Harlowton to Avery, Idaho, creating the first major mainline electrification in America.

articles.title: Sodbusters and the Railroad Land Promoters
articles.excerpt: The Northern Pacific sold its land grant acres to homesteaders with promotional literature promising adequate rainfall. The drought years of the 1910s proved otherwise.

articles.title: The Chinese Forerunners of the Northern Pacific
articles.excerpt: An estimated 15,000 Chinese laborers graded the Northern Pacific right-of-way through Montana's mountains in the 1880s, building the Chinatowns of Helena and Missoula.

articles.title: The Iron Horse and the Blackfeet Nation
articles.excerpt: The Great Northern Railway crossed Blackfeet territory under an 1895 agreement that ceded the mineral strip along the Rocky Mountain Front.

articles.title: See America First: The Great Northern and Glacier Park
articles.excerpt: James J. Hill's Great Northern Railway created Glacier National Park's tourism industry with the 'See America First' campaign and a chain of grand lodges.

articles.title: The St. Paul Pass Tunnel
articles.excerpt: The Milwaukee Road bored 8,771 feet through the Bitterroot Range at St. Paul Pass, completing the key engineering achievement of its Pacific Extension.

articles.title: The Longest Ghost: Abandoning the Milwaukee Road
articles.excerpt: When the Milwaukee Road abandoned its Pacific Extension in 1980, it left the longest ghost railroad in America stretching across Montana.

articles.title: The End of the Steamboat Era at Fort Benton
articles.excerpt: The arrival of the Great Northern Railway at Fort Benton in 1887 ended the steamboat trade that had sustained the farthest inland port in North America.

articles.title: Forty-Five Hours Through the Mountains: The Empire Builder
articles.excerpt: The Empire Builder, inaugurated in 1929, remains America's most popular long-distance train, running daily from Chicago to Seattle through Montana.

articles.title: The Richest Hill on Earth: Butte, 1914
articles.excerpt: The dynamiting of the Butte Miners Union Hall on June 13, 1914, ended thirty years of union recognition and began six years of National Guard occupation.

articles.title: A Town Every Ten Miles: The Northern Pacific and the Making of Montana
articles.excerpt: The Northern Pacific platted towns across Montana in the 1880s, including Billings, Miles City, and Glendive, establishing the urban geography of the state.

articles.title: The War of the Copper Kings
articles.excerpt: Marcus Daly, William A. Clark, and F. Augustus Heinze fought for control of Butte's copper from the 1880s through 1906, with railroads as weapons of war.

points.name: Smith Mine (Bear Creek)
points.description: Site of the Smith Mine disaster, February 27, 1943. An explosion killed 74 miners.

points.name: Red Lodge Depot (Montana, Wyoming and Southern RR)
points.description: Southern terminus of the coal branch line from Billings.

points.name: Custer Creek Train Wreck Site
points.description: Site of the June 19, 1938 wreck of the Milwaukee Road's Olympian passenger train. 47 passengers killed.

points.name: Northern Pacific Completion Site, Gold Creek
points.description: Site of the Northern Pacific Railroad completion ceremony, September 8, 1883.

points.name: Butte — BA&P Eastern Terminus
points.description: Eastern terminus of the Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway at the Butte mine yards.

points.name: Anaconda Copper Smelter
points.description: The Anaconda Copper smelter, western terminus of the BA&P. The 585-foot Anaconda Stack still stands.

points.name: Harlowton — Jawbone Railroad Western Terminus
points.description: Western terminus of the Montana Railroad (the 'Jawbone'), built by Richard Harlow.

points.name: Lewistown — Jawbone Railroad Eastern Terminus
points.description: Eastern terminus of the Jawbone Railroad at Lewistown, the largest city in central Montana.

points.name: Marias Pass Summit
points.description: Marias Pass, elevation 5,213 ft — the lowest crossing of the Continental Divide in the northern Rocky Mountains.

points.name: John Frank Stevens Monument
points.description: Monument to John Frank Stevens at Marias Pass, honoring his solo winter discovery of the pass in 1889.

points.name: Harlowton — Eastern End of Rocky Mountain Electrification
points.description: Harlowton was the eastern end of the Milwaukee Road's Rocky Mountain Division electrification (1915).

points.name: Billings — Northern Pacific Land Office
points.description: The NP's land office here sold railroad grant lands to homesteaders lured by promotional literature.

points.name: Livingston — Northern Pacific Division Point
points.description: Livingston was the Northern Pacific's division point for the Yellowstone branch. The 1902 depot still stands.

points.name: Helena — Chinese Railroad Worker Community
points.description: Helena's Chinatown was established by Chinese workers who graded the Northern Pacific right-of-way.

points.name: Missoula — Northern Pacific Mountain Division
points.description: Chinese laborers performed much of the grading through the Clark Fork canyon. The Missoula NP depot (1901) still stands.

points.name: Browning — Blackfeet Reservation (Great Northern Route)
points.description: Browning is the seat of the Blackfeet Nation. The Great Northern crossed Blackfeet territory under an 1895 agreement.

points.name: Cut Bank — Great Northern / Blackfeet Territory
points.description: The Great Northern's Hi-Line route bisected Blackfeet hunting territory.

points.name: East Glacier Park Station
points.description: Gateway to Glacier Park Lodge. Still an active Amtrak Empire Builder stop (seasonal).

points.name: Whitefish Station (Empire Builder)
points.description: Western gateway to Glacier National Park. The 1927 depot is on the National Register.

points.name: St. Paul Pass Tunnel — East Portal (Montana)
points.description: East portal of the St. Paul Pass (Taft) Tunnel. Now the eastern trailhead of the Route of the Hiawatha.

points.name: St. Paul Pass Tunnel — West Portal (Idaho)
points.description: West portal of the St. Paul Pass Tunnel near Avery, Idaho.

points.name: Harlowton — Milwaukee Road Abandonment Point
points.description: When the railroad abandoned its western lines in 1980, the tracks were pulled up. The Milwaukee Road depot still stands.

points.name: Fort Benton — Missouri River Levee
points.description: Fort Benton's levee — the farthest inland port in North America. The levee and historic district are preserved.

points.name: Fort Benton — Great Northern Depot
points.description: The Great Northern Railway reached Fort Benton in 1887, ending the steamboat trade.

points.name: Whitefish — Empire Builder Station
points.description: Primary Montana stop for Amtrak's Empire Builder, running Chicago to Seattle/Portland.

points.name: Butte — Miners Union Hall Site (1914 Riot)
points.description: Site of the Butte Miners Union Hall, dynamited June 13, 1914. The site is now a parking lot.

points.name: Granite Mountain / Speculator Mine Memorial
points.description: Site of the Granite Mountain / Speculator Mine fire, June 8, 1917. 168 miners killed.

points.name: Billings — NP-Platted Town, 1882
points.description: Billings was platted by the Northern Pacific in 1882 and named for railroad president Frederick Billings.

points.name: Miles City — Northern Pacific Division Point
points.description: Miles City grew around the Northern Pacific's arrival in 1881. Named for General Nelson Miles.

points.name: Glendive — Northern Pacific Division Point
points.description: Glendive was established by the Northern Pacific in 1881 as a division point.

points.name: Butte — Copper Kings Mine District
points.description: Where Marcus Daly, William A. Clark, and F. Augustus Heinze fought for control of the world's richest copper deposits.

points.name: Anaconda — Daly's Smelter and Company Town
points.description: Marcus Daly's company town, built around his copper smelter in 1884.

points.name: Great Falls — Boston and Montana Smelter
points.description: Site of the Boston and Montana Company's smelter, the second-largest copper operation in Montana.

routes.name: Montana, Wyoming and Southern RR — Coal Branch
routes.description: The coal branch running from Billings south through Red Lodge to the Bear Creek mines. Operated from 1898 until the Smith Mine closed after the 1943 disaster.

routes.name: Milwaukee Road — Eastern Montana (Miles City to Harlowton corridor)
routes.railroad: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad
routes.description: The Milwaukee Road's main line across eastern Montana. The Olympian and Columbian passenger trains ran this route.

routes.name: Northern Pacific Railway — Montana Main Line
routes.description: The Northern Pacific transcontinental main line across Montana, completed September 8, 1883.

routes.name: Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
routes.description: The 26-mile BA&P connecting Butte mines to the Anaconda smelter. First major electrified railroad in the US (1913).

routes.name: Montana Railroad (The Jawbone) — Lombard to Lewistown
routes.description: Richard Harlow's 'Jawbone Railroad' running from Lombard through Harlowton to Lewistown. Built 1900-1903 on credit and promises.

routes.name: Milwaukee Road — Rocky Mountain Division (Electrified, 1915-1974)
routes.railroad: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad
routes.description: The Milwaukee Road's electrified Rocky Mountain Division from Harlowton, MT to Avery, ID. 440 miles of 3,000V DC overhead wire.

routes.name: Great Northern Railway — Glacier Park Corridor
routes.description: The Great Northern's route through Glacier National Park's southern edge, from Cut Bank through East Glacier and Marias Pass to Whitefish.

routes.name: Milwaukee Road — Abandoned Montana Right-of-Way (1980)
routes.railroad: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad
routes.description: The Milwaukee Road's abandoned right-of-way across Montana. Tracks pulled up 1980.

routes.name: Empire Builder Route — Montana (Great Northern / BNSF Hi-Line)
routes.description: The Empire Builder's route across Montana on the former Great Northern Hi-Line. Active Amtrak service daily.
